hero.headline: How does Iowa termination law and at-will exceptions work?
hero.subhead: At-will with case-law exceptions, not a closed statutory list. The IWPCA final-pay rule is gentler than Arizona's 7-day clock, until you read what double-wages-plus-fees looks like on a $5,000 dispute.

at_a_glance.exception_basis: Case law — open public-policy tort (Springer v. Weeks & Leo Co., Iowa 1988)

is_iowa_at_will.question: Is Iowa really an at-will employment state?
is_iowa_at_will.answer_first: Yes. Iowa at-will employment rests on common law, not a codified statute. Either party can end the relationship at any time, for any reason not prohibited by law or contract.
is_iowa_at_will.body: Iowa does not have a statute equivalent to Arizona's Employment Protection Act (AEPA) that locks at-will into legislation while listing exceptions. Iowa's exceptions come from case law. The Iowa Supreme Court established the public-policy tort in Springer v. Weeks & Leo Co., 429 N.W.2d 558 (Iowa 1988), and case law has extended that doctrine in specific directions since.

at_will_exceptions.question: What are the exceptions to at-will employment in Iowa?
at_will_exceptions.answer_first: Iowa recognises five categories of at-will exception, all case-law or statute-based. There is no closed legislative list: plaintiffs can argue a public-policy anchor from any Iowa statute or the Iowa Constitution.

exceptions.name: Workers' compensation retaliation
exceptions.scope: Cannot discharge or threaten discharge because an employee filed or intends to file a workers' compensation claim.

exceptions.name: Iowa Civil Rights Act (ICRA) discrimination
exceptions.scope: Applies to employers with 4+ employees. Protected classes: race, colour, creed, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, national origin, religion, age (18+), disability, familial status.

exceptions.name: Jury duty retaliation
exceptions.scope: Cannot discharge an employee for responding to a jury summons or serving on a jury.

exceptions.name: Military service retaliation
exceptions.scope: Cannot discharge or disadvantage an employee for past, present, or future military service.

exceptions.name: Public-employee whistleblower
exceptions.scope: Protects state employees who in good faith report a violation of law. Does not apply directly to private-sector employees — private-sector protection must be argued as a common-law public-policy tort.

exceptions.name: Implied contract (handbook)
exceptions.basis: Springer v. Weeks & Leo Co. (Iowa 1988) and progeny
exceptions.scope: Handbook language promising 'termination only for cause' or 'progressive discipline' may create an implied employment contract. Iowa courts apply objective-expectation test.

iowa_civil_rights_act.question: How does the Iowa Civil Rights Act change your termination exposure?
iowa_civil_rights_act.answer_first: The Iowa Civil Rights Act covers employers with 4 or more employees. Federal Title VII requires 15. Iowa state anti-discrimination protection applies to far more employers than the federal statute.

final_pay_iwpca.question: What is the Iowa final-pay rule under the Iowa Wage Payment Collection Act?
final_pay_iwpca.answer_first: Iowa Code § 91A.4 requires wages to be paid on the next regular payday for the pay period in which the employee's last day fell. Iowa does not impose a separate, accelerated pay deadline for involuntary terminations.
final_pay_iwpca.penalty_description: Double the unpaid wages plus reasonable attorney's fees and costs if employer wilfully fails to pay

mass_layoffs.question: What about mass layoffs in Iowa?
mass_layoffs.answer_first: Iowa has no state mini-WARN Act. Federal WARN — 29 U.S.C. § 2101 — is the only advance-notice statute that applies to Iowa layoffs.

how_teamed_handles.question: How Teamed runs Iowa terminations end to end

faq_schema.q: Is Iowa an at-will employment state?
faq_schema.a: Yes. Iowa is an at-will employment state under common law. Either party can end the employment relationship at any time, for any reason not barred by statute, contract, or a recognised public-policy exception. Iowa has no statute that codifies at-will employment — the doctrine rests on case law, including the Iowa Supreme Court's decision in Springer v. Weeks & Leo Co. (Iowa 1988).

faq_schema.q: What are the exceptions to at-will employment in Iowa?
faq_schema.a: Iowa's public-policy exceptions are case-law based, not a closed statutory list. Recognised exceptions include: workers' compensation retaliation (Iowa Code § 85.18), discrimination under the Iowa Civil Rights Act (Iowa Code Ch. 216, covering employers with 4 or more employees), jury-duty retaliation (Iowa Code § 607A.45), military-service retaliation (Iowa Code Ch. 29A), and whistleblowing by state employees (Iowa Code § 70A.28). An implied contract from handbook language can also override at-will status.

faq_schema.q: When must you pay a terminated employee in Iowa?
faq_schema.a: Under the Iowa Wage Payment Collection Act (Iowa Code § 91A.4), wages are due on the next regular payday for the pay period in which the employee's last day fell. Iowa does not impose a special accelerated pay deadline for involuntary terminations. The employee must give written demand before suing; the employer then has 7 days to cure before litigation exposure under Iowa Code § 91A.10 attaches.

faq_schema.q: What is the penalty for unpaid wages in Iowa?
faq_schema.a: Under Iowa Code § 91A.8, if an employer wilfully fails to pay wages due, the employee can recover the unpaid wages plus liquidated damages equal to the unpaid amount — effectively double the shortfall — plus reasonable attorney's fees and costs. A $5,000 wage dispute can become $10,000 in statutory exposure plus attorney's fees before the case reaches a judge.

faq_schema.q: How long does an Iowa employee have to file a discrimination claim?
faq_schema.a: Iowa is a deferral state, so an EEOC federal discrimination charge can be filed within 300 days of the alleged discriminatory act. The Iowa Civil Rights Commission (ICRC) handles state-level claims under the Iowa Civil Rights Act and operates on the same 300-day timeline. Plaintiffs often dual-file with both bodies, running both timelines in parallel.
